content.text: Subword tokenization is the dominant strategy for converting raw text into token sequences for modern language models. Rather than splitting on whitespace (whole-word tokenization) or operating on individual characters, subword algorithms find a middle ground: common words stay intact while rare or compound words are split into meaningful fragments. The result is a vocabulary of a few tens of thousands of tokens that covers any input—including novel words, code identifiers, and multilingual text—with bounded vocabulary size.

Subword tokenization is a data-driven procedure that learns a vocabulary from a training corpus and then applies it at inference time to split arbitrary text into sub-word units. The three dominant algorithms—Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE), WordPiece, and Unigram/SentencePiece—all share the same goal but differ in how they decide which subwords to merge or retain.

BPE starts from individual characters (or bytes) and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair until a target vocabulary size is reached. WordPiece uses a similar bottom-up merge but selects pairs that maximise the likelihood of the training data under a language model, using the ratio P(AB)/(P(A)·P(B)). Unigram/SentencePiece takes the opposite direction: it starts with a large seed vocabulary and prunes tokens whose removal has the least impact on data likelihood.

At inference, the trained tokenizer encodes any string deterministically (BPE, WordPiece) or probabilistically (Unigram) into a sequence of integer token IDs. The model sees only IDs; the original string is reconstructed by a detokenizer. This two-step encode/decode process is invisible to callers of high-level APIs but becomes critical when you inspect model internals or manipulate token sequences directly.

The tokenizer is the interface between raw text and the model. Every prompt you send to an LLM is first tokenized; every completion is detokenized before you see it. Token boundaries affect everything: context window usage (longer tokens = fewer tokens per character = more context), model performance on rare words, code handling, and multilingual coverage.

For agentic systems the stakes are higher. Tool call arguments, structured JSON, URLs, and code snippets all pass through the tokenizer. An agent that misunderstands how its tokenizer splits a function name or a JSON key may produce subtly incorrect structured output. Understanding subword tokenization lets you write prompts that are token-efficient and avoid pathological splits that confuse the model.
30k–100k tokens cover essentially any input including rare words, proper nouns, and novel identifiers.
unknown words are handled by splitting them into known subword pieces rather than emitting an UNK token.
common words map to a single token, so most English prose compresses to roughly 0.75 tokens per character (~1.3 chars/token).
byte-level BPE and SentencePiece handle any Unicode script without language-specific rules.
keywords, identifiers, and operators often have dedicated tokens due to high corpus frequency in training data.
token ID 1042 means different strings in different tokenizers. Never pass IDs across model boundaries.
the tokenizer vocabulary is fixed at training time and cannot be extended without retraining.
languages underrepresented in the training corpus tokenize less efficiently (more tokens per word).
BPE and WordPiece differ in how they treat leading spaces; this affects round-trip fidelity.
Unigram tokenizers can assign different segmentations to the same string, complicating caching.

BPE training initialises the vocabulary with all bytes (256 entries for byte-level BPE) or all characters. It then repeats: count all adjacent byte/character pair frequencies in the corpus, merge the most frequent pair into a new token, add it to the vocabulary. This continues until the vocabulary reaches the target size (e.g., 50,257 for GPT-2).

At inference, the tokenizer applies the learned merge rules in the same order as training. The input string is first split into characters, then merges are applied greedily from highest-priority to lowest.

import tiktoken

# GPT-4 uses cl100k_base (BPE with 100,277 tokens)
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

# Encode a string
tokens = enc.encode("unhappiness")
print(tokens)        # [1904, 31194, 2136]  (3 tokens)
print([enc.decode([t]) for t in tokens])  # ['un', 'happ', 'iness']

# Encode with special tokens allowed
tokens2 = enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special={"<|endoftext|>"})
print(tokens2)  # [100257]

# Count tokens before sending to API
def count_tokens(text: str, model: str = "gpt-4o") -> int:
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    return len(enc.encode(text))

print(count_tokens("Hello, world!"))  # 4
WordPiece tokenization marks continuation subwords with a
prefix. The tokenizer first tries to match the longest known token starting from the current position. If the full word is unknown, it falls back to progressively shorter prefixes.

from transformers import BertTokenizer

tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

# WordPiece splits on word boundaries first, then subwords
tokens = tokenizer.tokenize("unhappiness")
print(tokens)   # ['un', '##happy', '##ness']
# Note: ## prefix marks continuation within a word

ids = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokens)
print(ids)   # [4895, 6057, 2791]  (BERT vocabulary)

# Full encode including [CLS] and [SEP] special tokens
encoded = tokenizer.encode("unhappiness", add_special_tokens=True)
print(encoded)  # [101, 4895, 6057, 2791, 102]
SentencePiece treats the input as a raw Unicode character stream with no pre-tokenization step. This makes it language-agnostic. The
(U+2581) character marks word boundaries, replacing the leading space convention.

import sentencepiece as spm

# Train a new SentencePiece model
spm.SentencePieceTrainer.train(
    input="corpus.txt",
    model_prefix="mymodel",
    vocab_size=8000,
    model_type="unigram",  # or 'bpe'
    character_coverage=0.9995,  # cover 99.95% of chars
)

sp = spm.SentencePieceProcessor()
sp.load("mymodel.model")

# Encode
tokens = sp.encode_as_pieces("unhappiness")
print(tokens)   # ['▁un', 'happiness']  (model-dependent)

ids = sp.encode_as_ids("unhappiness")
print(ids)      # [42, 3017]

# Decode back
text = sp.decode_pieces(tokens)
print(text)     # 'unhappiness'

# SentencePiece via HuggingFace (e.g. T5)
from transformers import T5Tokenizer
t5_tok = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained("t5-small")
print(t5_tok.tokenize("unhappiness"))
# ['▁un', 'happiness']

attrs.alt: Subword tokenization algorithm comparison: BPE (frequency-based merging), WordPiece (likelihood-based merging with ## continuation prefix), and SentencePiece/Unigram (top-down probabilistic pruning, ▁ word-boundary marker)

content.text: The three algorithm families share a common goal — a fixed vocabulary that covers open text — but differ in how they select and mark subword boundaries. BPE is deterministic and frequency-driven. WordPiece is likelihood-driven with explicit word pre-splitting and the
marker for continuations. SentencePiece/Unigram processes raw Unicode without a pre-tokenizer, using
as its word-boundary marker, and is the only one that can produce multiple valid segmentations for a single input string.
when building any system that sends text to or receives text from an LLM; understanding the tokenizer is not optional.
know your tokenizer's behaviour to write token-efficient prompts and avoid splitting critical terms.
count tokens before API calls to avoid truncation or overspend.
the tokenizer is frozen during fine-tuning; understand which vocabulary it uses.
prefer SentencePiece-based models for non-Latin scripts due to better Unicode handling.
mix token IDs from different tokenizers — they are not interchangeable.
Token counting for API cost estimation

import tiktoken
from typing import Sequence

def estimate_cost(
    messages: list[dict],
    model: str = "gpt-4o",
    input_price_per_1m: float = 2.50,
    output_price_per_1m: float = 10.00,
    expected_output_tokens: int = 500,
) -> dict:
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)

    # Count input tokens (simplified; production code must account for message overhead)
    input_text = " ".join(m["content"] for m in messages)
    input_tokens = len(enc.encode(input_text))

    # Add per-message overhead (~4 tokens each + 2 for reply priming)
    input_tokens += 4 * len(messages) + 2

    input_cost = (input_tokens / 1_000_000) * input_price_per_1m
    output_cost = (expected_output_tokens / 1_000_000) * output_price_per_1m

    return {
        "input_tokens": input_tokens,
        "expected_output_tokens": expected_output_tokens,
        "estimated_cost_usd": round(input_cost + output_cost, 6),
    }

messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Summarise the French Revolution in 3 sentences."},
]
print(estimate_cost(messages))

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

def show_splits(text: str, model_name: str) -> None:
    tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    tokens = tok.tokenize(text)
    ids = tok.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokens)
    print(f"Model: {model_name}")
    print(f"Input: {text!r}")
    for token, tid in zip(tokens, ids):
        print(f"  {tid:>6}  {token!r}")
    print(f"  Total: {len(tokens)} tokens")

# Compare how BPE (GPT-2) vs WordPiece (BERT) splits the same string
show_splits("tokenization", "gpt2")
# Model: gpt2
#   37435  'token'
#    1634  'ization'
#   Total: 2 tokens

show_splits("tokenization", "bert-base-uncased")
# Model: bert-base-uncased
#   19204  'token'
#    3989  '##ization'
#   Total: 2 tokens

# Notice: same split, different IDs, different ## convention
Code identifiers often tokenize differently from natural language. Understanding this helps when writing code-focused prompts or analysing model attention patterns.

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")  # GPT-4 family

identifiers = [
    "calculate_embedding_distance",
    "calculateEmbeddingDistance",
    "CalculateEmbeddingDistance",
    "calc_emb_dist",
]

for ident in identifiers:
    tokens = enc.encode(ident)
    pieces = [enc.decode([t]) for t in tokens]
    print(f"{ident:<35} -> {len(tokens):>2} tokens: {pieces}")

# calculate_embedding_distance    ->  5 tokens: ['calc', 'ulate', '_embedding', '_distance']
# calculateEmbeddingDistance      ->  4 tokens: ['calculate', 'Embedding', 'Distance']  (CamelCase often fewer tokens)
# CalculateEmbeddingDistance      ->  4 tokens: ['Calculate', 'Embedding', 'Distance']
# calc_emb_dist                   ->  5 tokens: ['calc', '_em', 'b', '_dist']

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

sentences = {
    "English": "The transformer architecture enables parallel sequence processing.",
    "Spanish": "La arquitectura transformadora permite el procesamiento paralelo de secuencias.",
    "Chinese": "Transformer架构支持并行序列处理。",
    "Arabic": "تُمكّن بنية المحول من معالجة التسلسل المتوازي.",
    "Korean": "트랜스포머 아키텍처는 병렬 시퀀스 처리를 가능하게 합니다.",
}

for lang, text in sentences.items():
    n = len(enc.encode(text))
    chars = len(text)
    print(f"{lang:<10}  {n:>3} tokens  {chars:>3} chars  {chars/n:.1f} chars/token")

# English      11 tokens   63 chars   5.7 chars/token
# Spanish      14 tokens   75 chars   5.4 chars/token
# Chinese      14 tokens   18 chars   1.3 chars/token  (costs ~4x more per char)
# Arabic       23 tokens   60 chars   2.6 chars/token
# Korean       23 tokens   39 chars   1.7 chars/token

An agentic customer support system routes tickets by language and builds per-ticket summaries. The pipeline first detects language, estimates token cost, then selects the appropriate summarisation model.

import tiktoken
from langdetect import detect

PRICE_PER_1M = {
    "gpt-4o": {"input": 2.50, "output": 10.00},
    "gpt-4o-mini": {"input": 0.15, "output": 0.60},
}

def route_and_estimate(ticket: str, system_prompt: str) -> dict:
    lang = detect(ticket)

    # Choose model: non-English tickets tend to use more tokens (less efficient tokenization)
    model = "gpt-4o" if lang == "en" else "gpt-4o"  # same model, but flag the extra cost

    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    input_tokens = len(enc.encode(system_prompt + ticket)) + 6  # message overhead

    # Estimate: summaries ~120 tokens
    output_tokens = 120
    prices = PRICE_PER_1M[model]
    cost = (input_tokens * prices["input"] + output_tokens * prices["output"]) / 1_000_000

    return {
        "language": lang,
        "model": model,
        "input_tokens": input_tokens,
        "estimated_cost_usd": round(cost, 6),
    }

ticket = "El pedido número 1234 no ha llegado después de 10 días."
system = "You are a support agent. Summarise this ticket in English in 2 sentences."
result = route_and_estimate(ticket, system)
print(result)
# {'language': 'es', 'model': 'gpt-4o', 'input_tokens': 38, 'estimated_cost_usd': 0.0000142}
ID 1042 in GPT-2's vocabulary is not the same string as ID 1042 in BERT's vocabulary. Always decode to string before crossing model boundaries.
English averages ~0.75 tokens/word, but code, JSON, and non-Latin scripts may use 3–5 tokens per 'word'.
Skipping token count before API calls:
sending a 200k-character document to a 128k-token model will truncate silently or raise an error depending on the client.
Using tokenizer from wrong model family:
to count tokens for a Claude model introduces estimation error (Claude uses a different BPE vocabulary).
vocabulary sizes vary by model and version. Use
rather than a magic constant like 50257.
Count tokens at the boundary between your code and the API, not after you have already constructed the full prompt.
Use the official tokenizer for your model family — tiktoken for OpenAI models, HuggingFace tokenizers for open models, Anthropic's tokenizer for Claude.
When building multilingual agents, benchmark token efficiency for each target language and budget accordingly.
Store raw UTF-8 strings in databases and message histories; only tokenize at inference time.
For fine-tuning datasets, ensure the tokenizer is the same one the base model used; do not swap tokenizers mid-training.
Tokenization is typically a CPU-bound preprocessing step. For batch inference, tokenize in parallel across CPU cores using the HuggingFace
tokenizer (Rust implementation) or tiktoken (Rust core). Both process megabytes of text in milliseconds and are thread-safe.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer
import torch

# Fast tokenizers use Rust under the hood
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased", use_fast=True)

# Batch encoding: returns padded tensors directly
batch = ["Hello world", "Subword tokenization is efficient", "Short"]
encoded = tokenizer(
    batch,
    padding=True,          # pad to longest sequence
    truncation=True,       # truncate to model max length
    max_length=128,
    return_tensors="pt",   # PyTorch tensors
)
print(encoded["input_ids"].shape)   # [3, 128]
print(encoded["attention_mask"])    # 1 for real tokens, 0 for padding
the reference BPE tokenizer for GPT-2/3/4/o models; pure Python API with Rust core; encodings include
supports all major algorithms (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram); fast (Rust) and slow (Python) variants; integrates with
Google's C++/Python library for Unigram and BPE; the reference implementation for T5, LLaMA, Gemma.
available via the Anthropic SDK; use it for accurate token counts when targeting Claude models.
HuggingFace's online tool at tokenizer.huggingface.co lets you explore tokenization behaviour interactively across models.

Token injection attacks exploit the gap between how humans read prompts and how the tokenizer segments them. A malicious instruction hidden in whitespace or unusual Unicode characters may tokenize into an innocuous-looking sequence. Always validate and sanitise user-supplied text before interpolating it into system prompts.

Token budget exhaustion is a reliability risk. If a user supplies an unexpectedly long document, tokenizing and sending it will either exceed the context window or consume an unbudgeted number of tokens. Always count tokens before making API calls; truncate or chunk input if it exceeds your budget.

Think of a subword tokenizer as a compression codec specialised for the language distribution in its training corpus. Frequent patterns get a short code (one token); rare patterns are spelled out as multiple codes. The vocabulary table is the codec dictionary. Just as you would not mix JPEG and PNG compression data, you cannot mix token IDs from different tokenizers.
Subword tokenization splits text into frequency-optimised fragments that balance vocabulary size against open-vocabulary coverage.
BPE merges by frequency (GPT family); WordPiece by likelihood ratio (BERT family); Unigram prunes by loss impact (T5, LLaMA, Gemma).
Token IDs are vocabulary-specific — never exchange them across model families.
English tokenizes at ~0.75 tokens/word; non-Latin scripts may use 2–5x more tokens for equivalent content.
Count tokens before every API call; truncate or chunk if over budget.
Use the official tokenizer for your model: tiktoken for OpenAI, sentencepiece/HF for open models, Anthropic SDK for Claude.
Store raw UTF-8 strings across pipeline stages; tokenize only at model boundaries.
